coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots with tunable negative charges for bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Fang
Song, Yuxiang
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Fu, Yijun
Huang, Jian
Li, Hao
Qu, Huihua
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Applying bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment is vital to human health. Nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) with high visible fluorescence and tunable negative charges were synthesized by a feasible hydrothermal reaction. The as-prepared NPCDs possess abundant surface functional groups, excellent photostability and bio-compatibility. Especially, the surface negative charges of NPCDs can be adjusted by changing the kind of the precursors. The obtained NPCDs as a fluorescent dye could selectively stain dead bacteria instead of live ones due to electrostatic repulsion since the cell walls of bacteria possess negative charges. The NPCDs with less negative charges provides a low accurate mortality rate due to a weaker repulsion, while NPCDs with the most negative charges give a comparable mortality rate with traditional plate counting method but consumes shorter time. Our study suggests that NPCDs with highly negative charges could serve as a precise and efficient fluorescent probe to determine the living state of bacteria, widening the path for bio-imaging application in bacterial viability assessment.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL stability and cytotoxicity test of NPCDs
Comparison of the surface charges of NPCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of NPCDs-1 (scale bar: 10 nm) with a typical size distribution; (b) FT-IR spectrum of NPCDs-1; (c) UV–vis spectrum of NPCDs-1; (d) PL spectra of NPCDs-1 with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 460 nm with 20 nm as an interval. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Full-scan XPS spectrum of NPCDs-1 with the atomic ratio of each element. High-resolution XPS spectra of NPCDs-1 (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Normalized fluorescent intensity of NPCDs-1 influenced by (a) time; (b) pH value and (c) ionic strength. (d) Absorbance-based viability assay to HeLa cells incubated with different concentrations of NPCDs-1 for 48 h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Fluorescence decay curve of NPCDs-1, NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4. (370 nm excitation, monitored with 440 nm narrow band-pass filter); (b) Zeta potential of NPCDs-1, NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4; (c) The relationship between zeta potential and nitrogen atomic percentage. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

LSCM images of E. coli. (a) Bright field, (b) dark field and (c) merged graphs of control group. (d) Bright field, (e) dark field and (f) merged graphs of live group. (g) Bright field, (h) dark field and (i) merged graphs of dead group. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) LSCM images of E. coli incubated with different types of NPCDs after being treated to partially dead. (b) Corresponding mortality rate determined by consistent type of NPCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots with tunable negative charges for bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Medical Sciences, Beijing University of Chinese Medicine, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou, 215123, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biology & Basic Medical Sciences, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou, 215123, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biology & Basic Medical Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Applying bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment is vital to human health. Nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) with high visible fluorescence and tunable negative charges were synthesized by a feasible hydrothermal reaction. The as-prepared NPCDs possess abundant surface functional groups, excellent photostability and bio-compatibility. Especially, the surface negative charges of NPCDs can be adjusted by changing the kind of the precursors. The obtained NPCDs as a fluorescent dye could selectively stain dead bacteria instead of live ones due to electrostatic repulsion since the cell walls of bacteria possess negative charges. The NPCDs with less negative charges provides a low accurate mortality rate due to a weaker repulsion, while NPCDs with the most negative charges give a comparable mortality rate with traditional plate counting method but consumes shorter time. Our study suggests that NPCDs with highly negative charges could serve as a precise and efficient fluorescent probe to determine the living state of bacteria, widening the path for bio-imaging application in bacterial viability assessment.

The bio-imaging application in bacterial viability assessment using NPCDs gives a comparable mortality rate with plate counting method.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The assessment of bacterial viability is vital to human health. Hence, it is of high privilege to develop a fast and simple route to assess bacterial viability [1–3]. Generally, an established plate counting method is the most widely applied way to distinguish live or dead bacteria, which can also give an accurate mortality rate [4]. However, several hours or days of bacteria incubation are required using this method. Considering the cell walls of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria possess negative charges, it is possible to apply bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment using a fluorescent dye with negative charges [5].

Nanomaterials are being widely studied in recent years [6–9]. Carbon dots (CDs), which are emerging as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial for their widespread applications in substantial areas, have aroused considerable attention owing to their dramatic properties including favorable photoluminescence (PL), excellent chemical stability and catalytic activity, water solubility and biocompatibility [10–14]. One of the most notable feature of CDs is that they can be easily surface functionalized, so that new dopant elements can be employed for the purpose of enhanced properties. The doped elements in CDs that can provide additional lone pairs of electrons or unoccupied orbital, such as nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P), are ideal dopants to improve electronic and optical properties of CDs. Moreover, N could introduce defect sites by replacing carbon atoms, providing potential active sites to enrich chemical activities of CDs [15–18]. A series of papers have reported versatile applications of doped CDs in photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, sensors, cell imaging etc., as well as the methods to synthesize CDs with N and/or P as dopants such as heating urea-derived C3N4 and ethylenediamine, pyrolysis of the mixture of urea and phosphoric acid, heating ammonium citrate etc. [19–23] However, to the best of our knowledge, studies of the relationship between the ratio of dopants and the surface charges of doped CDs have not been reported so far. Moreover, the doped CDs with negative charges may be an ideal dye via bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment.

Herein, we introduced that the obtained NPCDs as a fluorescent dye could selectively stain dead bacteria instead of live ones due to electrostatic repulsion, while NPCDs with more negative charges give a comparable mortality rate with traditional plate counting method but consumes shorter time. The desired NPCDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal reaction of ethylenediamine and yeast extract. Compared with existing methods, the yeast extract we used is directly derived from yeast fungus, leading to an excellent bio-compatibility (over 80% alive with 500 μg/mL). The as-prepared NPCDs showed strong fluorescence and negative charges, which can be tuned by changing the involved precursor. More importantly, after a short time of incubation with E. coli (2 h), NPCDs can selectively stain dead bacteria rather than live ones, while the more negative surface charges gave the more accurate mortality rate (36.1%), which could compete with the traditional time-consuming plate counting method (37.6%). It is suggested that NPCDs with highly negative surface charges could serve as an efficient and accurate fluorescent probe to determine the exact mortality rate of bacteria in a short time, and widen the path to apply bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment.
All the chemical reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and they were used as received. The HeLa cell line was purchased from the Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Science.

Transmission electron microscopy images were obtained from a FEI/Philips Tecnai G2 F20 TWIN TEM. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were characterized by a Bruker Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Hyperion), while the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) measurement used a Perkin Elmer UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired using a Horiba Jobin Yvon (Fluoro Max-4) luminescence spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Dynamic light scattering and zeta potential of NPCDs was measured using a ZEN3690 zetasizer (Malvern, U.K.). Laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) images were collected by a Laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope (Leica, TCS-SP5).

All NPCDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal reaction of the precursors at 120 °C for 1 day to form original NPCDs solutions directly, and labeled as NPCDs-1, NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4 corresponding to solution-1, solution-2, solution-3 and solution-4, respectively. After that, the original NPCDs solutions were dialyzed using a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 500) to remove the impurities and ions (Na+, K+ and Cl−). The obtained NPCDs solutions were stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

To further study the relation between nitrogen atomic ratio and surface charge of NPCDs, CDs without doped nitrogen (CDs-0) were synthesized via a reported method by directly heating citric acid [24]. 2 g citric acid was added into a beaker and heated at 200 °C for 30 min. The liquated citric acid was neutralized to pH = 7 using 10 mg/mL NaOH solution to obtain an orange solution. The as-prepared CDs-0 solution was stored at 4 °C and the zeta potential of it was measured along with NPCDs-1, NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4.

The HeLa cell line was cultured in the standard medium at 37 °C in 5% CO2. Cells were incubated in a 96-well plate for 2 days. NPCDs were diluted to a series of known concentrations and then added to the cells. After incubation for 2 days, the relative viabilities of the cell were measured using colorimetric 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays to assess the metabolic activity of cells treated as described above. The cells were lysed with acidulated sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). Absorbance was determined at 570 nm by a microplate reader (Bio-Rad 680, USA). At least three independent experiments were carried out to obtain the accurate data.

E. coli was shaking cultured at 180 rpm in lysogeny broth (LB) medium at 37 °C and obtained in exponential phase of cell cycle with the value of optical density at 600 nm (OD600) reaching 0.6–0.8. Then E. coli was washed with sterile water and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 3 min for 3 times. Alive E. coli was boiled at 60 °C for 1 h to obtain dead ones, whereas 10 min for partially dead ones. E. coli was dispersed in the solutions of NPCDs at 37 °C after boiling treatment, and shaking cultured for another 2 h. Before LSCM analysis, all the samples of E. coli were washed with sterile water and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 6 times in order to remove NPCDs that did not enter the bacteria.

Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image and related size distribution of NPCDs-1, demonstrating that NPCDs-1 are uniformly dispersed with their diameters in the range of 1–6 nm. The FT-IR spectrum of NPCDs-1 is exhibited in Fig. 1b. The typical absorption peaks at 3435 cm−1 and 3268 cm−1 can be assigned to stretching vibrations of OH and NH of amino groups, respectively. The peak at 2927 cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of methylene, while a characteristic peak of CO stretching vibrations can be observed at 1632 cm−1. The peak located at 1393 cm−1 can be attributed to stretching vibrations of CN, and a peak centered at 1086 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC [25–28]. Fig. 1c shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NPCDs-1. The absorption peak located at ∼280 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition of CC, while the peak at ∼340 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition of carbonyl/amine functional groups [19]. The FT-IR and UV–Vis spectra of NPCDs-1 demonstrate that plenty of hydrophilic functional groups exist on the surface of NPCDs-1, leading to an excellent aqueous dispersibility. As shown in Fig. 1d, NPCDs-1 can emit a strong fluorescence under the excitation from 300 nm to 460 nm, with the maximum fluorescent intensity obtained at 360 nm excitation. As the excitation wavelength elevates, the resulting fluorescence intensity gradually red shifted, which may be caused by defect luminescence [22]. TEM images, FT-IR spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4 are similar to those of NPCDs-1 shown in Fig. S1–S3, respectively. Digital photographs of NPCDs-1, NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4 under sunlight as well as UV light (365 nm) are shown in Fig. S4.

Fig. 2 a depicts the full-scan XPS spectrum of NPCDs-1, from which we can observe four peaks located at 284.8 eV, 399.2 eV, 531.0 eV and 133.1 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and P 2p in NPCDs-1, respectively. In addition, NPCDs-1 contain 60.95 at% of carbon, 28.95 at% of oxygen, 8.42 at% nitrogen and 1.68 at% of phosphorus, according to XPS results. High-resolution XPS spectra of C, N and O are collected to illustrate the detailed bonding formation during the hydrothermal reaction process, and the results are presented in Fig. 2b–d. The partial XPS spectrum of C 1s can be divided into three peaks centered at 284.8 eV (sp2 C), 286.1 eV (CO and/or CN) and 287.8 eV (CO). Two peaks at 399.5 eV and 401.1 eV can be observed in high resolution N 1s spectrum, revealing the existence of CN and CN bonding. The high resolution O 1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks located at 530.8 eV, 532.2 eV and 535.4 eV, which can be assigned to CO, CO and COO−, respectively [29,30]. The XPS analysis suggests that N and P successfully doped with C and O of the carbon core. Similar XPS analysis of NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4 can be observed in Fig. S5–S7, respectively, indicating that all four types of NPCDs have a similar carbon core and surface functional groups. The main components of yeast extract were measured using an energy dispersive X-ray fluorescence spectrometer (EDX), and the result is presented in Fig. S8. Signals contributed from C, N, O, Na, P, Cl and K can be observed, whereas Na, Cl and K could be removed by dialysis process. The EDX result is consistent with XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL stability and cytotoxicity test of NPCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PL stability of NPCDs-1 with the change of time, pH value and ionic strength was investigated, and the results are displayed in Fig. 3 a–c. The fluorescence emitted from NPCDs-1 can maintain the same level for at least 24 h at room temperature. In addition, the normalized intensity of NPCDs-1 merely changes when pH value increases from 5 to 10. As the concentration of NaCl increases from 0 to 6 mol/L, the normalized intensity barely varies within the range of 5%. In summary, NPCDs-1 have a remarkable photostability, and there is no visible photobleaching under the influence of time (0–24 h), pH value (5–10) and ionic strength (NaCl concentration 0–6 mol/L).

HeLa cell line was used to test the cytotoxicity of NPCDs-1. NPCDs-1 of varied concentrations were added into the HeLa cells incubated in 96-well plate for 2 days. At the same time, a standard assay was set to evaluate the influence of NPCDs-1 on the metabolic activities of HeLa cells. The percentage of cells alive after 2-day incubation with NPCDs-1 is shown in Fig. 3d, from which we can see that over 80% cells are alive even with the highest concentration of NPCDs-1 (500 μg/mL), revealing the low toxicity of NPCDs-1 in vitro. Graphs of PL stability and cytotoxicity test of NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4 are exhibited in Fig. S9–S11, respectively, which demonstrate that they have a similar photostability and cytotoxicity to that of NPCDs-1. It is worth noting that PL of NPCDs-2 slightly decreases with time, which may be attributed to agglomeration according to the measured zeta potential of NPCDs-2 (−5.02 mV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the surface charges of NPCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a exhibits the comparison of fluorescence decay curve of NPCDs-1, NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4. (370 nm excitation, monitored with 440 nm narrow band-pass filter). NPCDs-1 have the longest fluorescence lifetime up to 2.59 ns. Fig. 4b discloses the exact value of zeta potential of NPCDs-1 (−24.6 mV), NPCDs-2 (−5.02 mV), NPCDs-3 (−17.7 mV) and NPCDs-4 (−15.5 mV). The relationship between zeta potential and nitrogen atomic percentage is revealed in Fig. 4c. Nitrogen atomic percentage of NPCDs-1, NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4 are 8.42 at%, 10.71 at%, 7.34 at% and 9.66 at% respectively, according to the XPS analysis above. It is worth noting that as nitrogen atomic percentage raises from 7 at% to 11 at%, the corresponding curve undergoes two processes, where the zeta potential decreases first to a minimum value about −25 mV at 8.25 at% (process 1), and then increases gradually (process 2). The zeta potential of CDs-0 was measured to be +0.91 mV as depicted in Fig. S12, which fits the curve well. There are abundant amino groups on the surface of NPCDs, which is a good electron donator and could enhance the adsorption capacity of protons [19]. At process 1, as the ratio of doped-nitrogen increases, more amino groups are introduced on the surface of NPCDs, leading to a denser electron cloud around NPCDs. Moreover, an amino group could adsorb one of the hydrogen atoms in a water molecule, making the bonding electrons between H and O slightly drift to O. Thus the rest OH group, which is still adsorbed on the surface of NPCDs, mildly possess negative charges. Both the two reasons may result in the decrease of zeta potential at process 1. However, as the ratio of doped-nitrogen continues increasing at process 2, exceeded amino groups are on the surface of NPCDs, and they are so close to each other that adsorption may occur among them, i. e. potential agglomeration phenomenon may take place. In addition, it is deduced that an antagonistic behavior may take place between two or more amino groups in competing for one water molecule. As a result, the efficiency of NPCDs to adsorb water molecules recedes, making the zeta potential increase at process 2. The stability of zeta potential depending on time was tested under room temperature, and the results are shown in Fig. S13. For NPCDs-2, the zeta potential decreased continuously during 24 h, which may also be attributed to agglomeration. Notably, doped-P merely influences the zeta potential because the atomic percentage of P is relatively small compared with that of N, and they are almost the same in NPCDs-1 (1.68%), NPCDs-2 (1.66%), NPCDs-3 (1.69%) and NPCDs-4 (1.66%) according to XPS analysis above.

Fluorometric detection method is a typical method to detect live and dead bacteria. Propidium monoazide (PMA) and propidium iodide (PI) are two most used commercial dyes for this method [31,32]. However, possible photo-bleaching and cytotoxicity of organic dyes are still problem [5]. Considering the obtained NPCDs-1 have the properties of high fluorescent intensity, excellent photostability and low toxicity, it was assumed that NPCDs-1 may have the potential to be applied in bio-imaging. Since the surface charge on the surface of NPCDs-1 is highly negative, we speculated that NPCDs-1 can selectively enter dead E. coli. The cell wall of E. coli contains lipopoly saccharide, which possess negative charges to keep the cation concentration in the bacteria suitable for living. Benefiting from this, NPCDs-1 are blocked from going inside E. coli due to the electrostatic repulsion of the same charges. When E. coli are dead, the cell wall loses control of the incoming substances, so NPCDs-1 can easily penetrate the cell wall ascribing their relatively small size if there is a concentration gradient.

Fig. 5 displays the LSCM images of E. coli, labeled as control, live and dead. The control group is E. coli incubated in sterile water after incubation in LB medium, while the live group contains E. coli cultured in NPCDs-1 solution. E. coli which were boiled at 60 °C for 1 h before incubated in NPCDs-1 are labeled as dead group. (Detailed incubation conditions can be observed in experimental section.) With 405 nm excitation, no visual fluorescence was emitted from E. coli in control group according to Fig. 5a–c, meaning that the bacteria themselves were not fluorescent. Also, there was no visible fluorescence of E. coli from live group, caused by the repellent charges. However, bright fluorescence can be observed from E. coli in dead group, which corresponds to our hypothesis.

Relative works have been reported by Xian-Wu Hua and Jingjing Yang, showing a remarkable ability to distinguish live and dead bacteria using CDs they obtained [5,33]. However, the carbonization temperature we used is much lower, leading to a small size and good dispersity of NPCDs. In addition, the determination of accurate mortality rate using CDs as fluorescent dyes is still ambiguous. Furthermore, we investigated if the value of zeta potential has influence on the effects of the bacterial viability assessment with a simultaneous mortality rate calculation. E. coli was boiled in 60 °C water bath for 10 min to obtain partially dead bacteria. Then, the E. coli samples were incubated with NPCDs-1, NPCDs-2, NPCDs-3 and NPCDs-4. (Detailed incubation conditions can be observed in experimental section.) A control group was set by incubating the sample in sterile water under the same conditions as above. Fig. 6 a shows the LSCM merged images, from which dead bacteria (fluorescent) and live ones (nonfluorescent) are distinguished. All NPCDs with negative surface charge have the possibility to selectively stain dead E. coli. However, NPCDs-1, which possess the most negative value, were capable to mark more dead bacteria than the other three NPCDs. To obtain a more accurate ratio of dead E. coli, the total number of bacteria and the number of bacteria with fluorescence were counted from five independent LSCM images of each NPCDs, and then took an average. The exact mortality rate was calculated following the equation below:
M o r t a l i t y
r a t e
=
n u m b e r
o f
b a c t e r i a
w i t h
f l u o r e s c e n c e
t o t a l
n u m b e r
o f
b a c t e r i a

The mortality rate of control group was determined according to established procedure using two E. coli samples with the same concentration. One of the sample was boiled in 60 °C water bath for 10 min, and no treatments was applied to the other. The mortality rate of the control group can be calculated via the plate counting method. The comparison between NPCDs of the ability to determine exact mortality rate is exhibited in Fig. 6b. NPCDs-1, which own the most negative surface charge, show the most accurate mortality rate, whereas NPCDs with less negative surface charges fail to give an accurate mortality rate. Considering the NPCDs are of relatively small size compared with E. coli, NPCDs with less negative surface charge have a greater chance to penetrate through the cell wall because of a weaker electrostatic repulsion. Notably, the accuracy of NPCDs-1 in evaluating bacterial viability can compete with the plate counting method, showing an error within 4%. Moreover, NPCDs-1 can stain dead bacteria within 2 h, which consumes quite less time than traditional plate incubation. In addition, the differentiation of Gram-positive bacteria and determination of its mortality rate were investigated with the same procedure of E. coli by using S. aureus and NPCDs-1. S. aureus as Gram-positive bacteria possesses teichoic acid in their cell walls, which functions similarly to lipopoly saccharide in E. coli. Thus, NPCDs-1 could be blocked from entering live S. aureus due to electrostatic repulsion, which is shown in Fig. S14. However, NPCDs-1 could still stain dead S. aureus and gave a mortality rate as 29.8% after 60 °C water bath for 10 min, which is comparable with plate counting methods (30.8%). The application of NPCDs-1 as a fluorescent dye have the potential to provide a faster and easier approach to assess bacterial viability than the traditional plate counting method.

We developed a feasible method to synthesize nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon dots by hydrothermal method. The as-prepared NPCDs exhibit high visible fluorescence, excellent photostability, low toxicity and negative charges. Especially, the negative charges of NPCDs can be tuned by changing the involved precursors. Moreover, for the first time, we investigated the relationship between the negative charges and nitrogen atomic percentage and discovered that the zeta potential showed a prior decrease before increasing, as nitrogen atomic percentage elevating from 7 at% to 11 at%. It is deduced that abundant amino groups on the surface of NPCDs provide dense electron cloud, as well as they can adsorb hydrogen atoms in water molecules, resulting in more negative charges. As nitrogen atomic ratio continues increasing, excess amino groups on the surface may undergo agglomeration and antagonistic behavior, leading to a weaker adsorption of protons from water molecules.

In addition, the obtained NPCDs could selectively stain dead E. coli rather than live ones due to electrostatic repulsion between NPCDs and cell walls of the bacteria. More importantly, NPCDs with the most negative surface charges could precisely stain the dead bacteria in a partially dead E. coli sample in a short time (2 h). The result (mortality rate = 36.1%) can compete with the result obtained from traditional plate counting method (mortality rate = 37.6%) but consumes less time. NPCDs with less negative charges fail to provide an accurate mortality rate because of a weaker electrostatic repulsion. Our findings suggest that NPCDs with highly negative surface charges could serve as an efficient fluorescent dye to provide a simple, fast and easy route to evaluate bacterial viability, widening the path to apply bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51422207, 51572179, 21471106, 21501126, 31671216), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161216) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.05.039.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free in situ discrimination of live and dead bacteria by surface-enhanced raman scattering

Core-shell upconversion nanoparticle@metal-organic framework nanoprobes for luminescent/magnetic dual-mode targeted imaging

The doping of reduced graphene oxide with nitrogen and its effect on the quenching of the material's photoluminescence

Fluorescent N-doped carbon dots for both cellular imaging and highly-sensitive catechol detection

Nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate

Carbon dot-based platform for simultaneous bacterial distinguishment and antibacterial applications
